parentQuestion: A patient has double vision when looking down (reading, stairs) and tilts their head to compensate. Which nerve is damaged?

options: Seeing two images of one object
scaffolds.explain: DIPLOPIA = seeing TWO images of a single object. It occurs when the eyes aren't properly aligned, so each eye sees a slightly different view that the brain can't merge into one image.

wrongExplains.0: Color vision problems are different (color blindness). Diplopia is about seeing double, not color changes.
wrongExplains.2: Blindness is complete vision loss. With diplopia, you can see — you just see double.
wrongExplains.3: Night-only vision (nyctalopia) is a different condition. Diplopia is double vision at any time.

scaffolds.q: The patient has trouble looking DOWN. Which activity would be especially difficult?
options: Reading a book or walking down stairs
scaffolds.explain: READING and WALKING DOWN STAIRS require looking down. If downward gaze is impaired, these activities cause double vision and difficulty.

wrongExplains.0: Looking at the ceiling requires upward gaze, not downward. Different muscles, different problem.
wrongExplains.2: Looking straight ahead uses balanced muscle activity. The problem here is specifically with looking down.
wrongExplains.3: Closing the eyes uses CN VII (facial). Eye movement down uses CN IV.

scaffolds.q: Which cranial nerve controls the superior oblique muscle for looking DOWN and inward?
scaffolds.explain: CN IV (trochlear) controls ONLY the superior oblique muscle. This muscle pulls the eye DOWN and rotates it INWARD (intorsion). CN IV damage = trouble looking down.

wrongExplains.0: CN III controls four eye muscles (up, down via inferior rectus, medial, inferior oblique) but NOT the superior oblique. The superior oblique is CN IV.
wrongExplains.2: CN VI controls the lateral rectus for looking OUTWARD (lateral gaze). It doesn't help with looking down.
wrongExplains.3: CN VII controls facial EXPRESSION. It has nothing to do with eye movement.

scaffolds.q: CN IV is unique because it controls how many eye muscles?
scaffolds.explain: CN IV controls ONLY ONE muscle — the superior oblique. It's a specialist nerve with a single dedicated job. This is different from CN III, which controls four muscles.

wrongExplains.1: CN IV controls just one muscle. You might be thinking of a different nerve.
wrongExplains.2: Four muscles are controlled by CN III (oculomotor). CN IV has just one.
wrongExplains.3: Six muscles are split between THREE nerves: CN III (four), CN IV (one), CN VI (one).

scaffolds.q: Why would a patient TILT THEIR HEAD to compensate for CN IV palsy?
options: Tilting compensates for the eye's inability to rotate properly (intorsion)
scaffolds.explain: The superior oblique normally ROTATES the eye inward (intorsion). If it can't, the patient tilts their head to mechanically align the eyes and reduce double vision. This is a classic compensatory sign.

wrongExplains.0: Tilting definitely helps! It's such a common compensation that doctors look for head tilt as a sign of CN IV palsy.
wrongExplains.2: Hearing uses CN VIII and isn't affected by head tilt for eye muscle problems.
wrongExplains.3: Head tilting doesn't heal nerves. It's a mechanical compensation to align vision, not a treatment.

scaffolds.q: Which way does a patient with RIGHT CN IV palsy typically tilt their head?
options: To the right (toward the affected side)
To the left (away from the affected side)
scaffolds.explain: The patient tilts AWAY from the affected side. With RIGHT CN IV palsy, they tilt LEFT. This compensates for the right eye's inability to intort and reduces diplopia.

wrongExplains.0: Tilting toward the affected side would worsen the problem, not help it.
wrongExplains.2: Backward tilt doesn't help with intorsion problems. The compensation is a lateral (sideways) tilt.
wrongExplains.3: Head tilt is one of the most reliable signs of CN IV palsy. Patients absolutely do compensate this way.

scaffolds.q: CN IV is also unique because it exits from which part of the brainstem?
options: Anterior (front) like all other cranial nerves
Posterior (back) — the only cranial nerve to do so
It doesn't come from the brainstem
scaffolds.explain: CN IV is the ONLY cranial nerve that exits from the POSTERIOR (dorsal) brainstem. All others exit anteriorly. This unique path makes it vulnerable to injury in certain conditions.

wrongExplains.0: That's where other nerves exit, but CN IV is the exception. It exits from the back.
wrongExplains.2: CN IV specifically exits from the posterior (dorsal) midbrain, not the sides.
wrongExplains.3: CN IV does come from the brainstem (midbrain), just from the back side uniquely.

scaffolds.q: Why is CN IV particularly vulnerable to HEAD TRAUMA?
options: It has the longest path inside the skull and is thin
scaffolds.explain: CN IV has the LONGEST intracranial course of any cranial nerve AND is the thinnest. During head trauma, shearing forces can stretch and damage this delicate, long nerve.

wrongExplains.0: CN IV is actually the THINNEST cranial nerve. Its small size makes it vulnerable.
wrongExplains.2: While inside the skull, it's not specially protected. Its long path through cerebrospinal fluid makes it vulnerable to movement injuries.
wrongExplains.3: CN IV does enter the skull — it has the longest path inside the skull of any cranial nerve.

scaffolds.q: To summarize: Double vision looking down + head tilt = damage to CN ___.
scaffolds.explain: CN IV (trochlear) palsy classic presentation: Double vision on DOWNWARD gaze + HEAD TILT compensation. This pattern is almost pathognomonic (uniquely identifying) for CN IV damage.

wrongExplains.0: CN III palsy shows ptosis, dilated pupil, and eye down-and-out. Different presentation.
wrongExplains.2: CN VI palsy shows inability to look OUTWARD (laterally). Different direction of gaze affected.
wrongExplains.3: CN VII affects facial expression. It doesn't affect eye movement at all.
